title: GPDE Workshop - The affine maximal surface equation and related topics
speaker: Xu-Jia Wang

proof_stages.label: Affine mean-curvature equation and invariances
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the affine metric and affine volume of a locally convex hypersurface, derives the graph Euler equation as a fourth-order affine mean-curvature equation, rewrites it as a coupled linearized Monge-Ampere/Monge-Ampere system, and records its affine invariances.

proof_stages.label: Variational first boundary value problem
proof_stages.summary: The first boundary conditions u=phi and Du=Dphi are relaxed to a convex variational problem with fixed boundary value and gradient-image constraint, and the main theorem asserts a unique maximizer that is smooth in the interior.

proof_stages.label: Existence and uniqueness of nonsmooth maximizers
proof_stages.summary: The affine volume functional is extended to nonsmooth convex functions, upper semicontinuity gives existence, and uniqueness follows by showing a maximizer has no singular Monge-Ampere mass and then invoking uniqueness for the Monge-Ampere Dirichlet problem.

proof_stages.label: Interior a priori estimates for strict convex solutions
proof_stages.summary: For smooth strictly convex solutions one obtains interior W^{4,p} and C^{4,alpha} estimates, using upper and lower determinant bounds, the Legendre transform, Caffarelli's Monge-Ampere regularity, and Holder estimates for the linearized Monge-Ampere equation.

proof_stages.label: Second boundary value problem as regularizing auxiliary problem
proof_stages.summary: A direct boundary problem prescribing u and w on the boundary is introduced and shown, under convexity and sign assumptions, to have a unique uniformly convex solution with enough global or boundary regularity to support the approximation argument.

proof_stages.label: Penalty approximation by smooth solutions
proof_stages.summary: The nonsmooth maximizer is approximated by smooth solutions of penalized affine mean-curvature equations on slightly enlarged domains, with large boundary-strip penalties chosen so that both the functions and their boundary gradient images converge correctly.

proof_stages.label: Strict convexity and final regularity conclusion
proof_stages.summary: Possible line segments in the graph of a maximizer are ruled out case by case using affine normalization, boundary convexity, Legendre-transform estimates, blow-up arguments, and Caffarelli strict-convexity theory, allowing the a priori estimates to yield interior smoothness.

examples.name: Euclidean minimal-surface analogy
examples.note: The classical minimal-surface equation and mean curvature as the Euler equation of area are used to explain the terminology and variational meaning of the affine maximal/mean-curvature equation, not to drive the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker invokes Euclidean minimal-surface theory only as an analogy for the affine equation and variational setup, without computing or checking anything in the Euclidean case

passes.reason: Euclidean minimal surfaces are invoked as the variational/zero-mean-curvature analogue for affine maximal surfaces, not to refute a claim.
Euclidean minimal surfaces are invoked as the area-critical/minimal analogue for the affine maximal equation, not to refute any claim.
Euclidean minimal surfaces are area-critical/minimizing variational objects used here only as an analogy for the affine equation, not to refute a claim.
Euclidean minimal surfaces are invoked only as the variational analogy for affine maximal surfaces, and structurally they are critical points of the Euclidean area functional.

Euclidean minimal surfaces are invoked only as the motivating analogue of affine maximal surfaces, and structurally they are stationary/critical points of the Euclidean area functional rather than generic hypersurfaces.
Euclidean minimal surfaces are invoked as the variational/zero-mean-curvature analogue for the affine equation, not to refute a claim.
The Euclidean minimal-surface analogy is used only as motivational comparison, and minimal surfaces are variationally extremal as critical points of the area functional.

The Euclidean minimal-surface equation is used only as an analogy/model for the affine equation, and minimal surfaces are structurally variationally extremal as area-critical/area-minimizing objects, not counterexamples.

The Euclidean minimal-surface equation is invoked only as a variational analogy for the affine equation, not to refute a claim; minimal surfaces are intrinsically extremal as critical/local area-minimizing objects.
Euclidean minimal surfaces are used only as the variational/geometric analogy for the affine equation, and are intrinsically area-critical objects rather than counterexamples.
Euclidean minimal surfaces are invoked only as an analogy for the affine maximal equation, and intrinsically they are area-critical/minimizing objects among surfaces.
Euclidean minimal surfaces are invoked only as a variational analogy, and structurally they are critical/minimizing objects for the Euclidean area functional.
